title: The Biggest Art Theft in History
summary: In 1990, two men dressed as police officers tricked a guard and stole 13 artworks from a museum in Boston, USA. The paintings are worth over $500 million. The thieves cut paintings from their frames, including works by famous artists like Vermeer and Rembrandt. The museum still has the empty frames hanging on the walls. The crime has never been solved, but a former FBI agent wrote a book naming the people he thinks did it. The museum owner, Isabella Stewart Gardner, wanted everything to stay the same forever, so the empty frames remind us of the stolen art. A reward of $10 million is offered for information.

keywords.explanation: stealing valuable paintings and other art
a building where art is kept for people to see
a U.S. police agency that investigates big crimes
money given to someone who helps find the stolen art

questions.question: What did the thieves pretend to be?
What is the reward for information?
Who wrote a book about the theft?
What still hangs in the museum?

background_read: The Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum is in Boston, USA. It was built like a fancy Italian palace. The owner, Isabella Stewart Gardner, loved art and wanted her collection to stay exactly as she arranged it forever. After she died in 1924, the museum kept everything the same. The empty frames remind visitors of the stolen art.
Article_Structure: WHO: Two thieves dressed as police officers.
WHAT: Stole 13 artworks worth over $500 million.
WHERE: Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum in Boston.
WHY: The theft is unsolved and the art is still missing.

perspectives.description: The museum wants the art back and offers a reward.
The FBI investigated but the case remains open.
